title: Cellulitis — Lower limb
oneLiner: Acute bacterial skin infection of the lower limb; common but can mimic limb-threatening conditions (necrotising fasciitis, DVT) and cause sepsis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Necrotising fasciitis (NF):** extreme pain out of proportion to skin changes, rapid progression, systemic toxicity, bullae/haemorrhagic blisters, gas on palpation/imaging. **The single finding:** pain *worse* than the visible skin suggests NF — this is the killer, not simple cellulitis.
- **Sepsis with shock:** high NEWS2, lactate >2 mmol/L, hypotension not responding to fluids. **The single finding:** lactate >4 + HR >110 despite 20 mL/kg fluid — needs urgent antibiotics and senior input.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** acute onset of unilateral lower limb redness, warmth, swelling, pain; often with fever/chills; usually no wound visible (but look for interdigital cracks, ulcers, trauma)
- **Key exam findings:** erythema with poorly defined border (vs well-defined in erysipelas); local warmth; tender to palpation; ± lymphangitic streaking or inguinal lymphadenopathy; check for bullae, pus, weeping; measure limb circumference for progression
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Deep vein thrombosis (DVT):** purple-red, non-tender swelling, Homan's sign unreliable — use Wells score (DVT likely >2 points); do D-dimer + ultrasound if low pretest probability
- **Venous stasis dermatitis (eczema):** bilateral, itchy, scaly, usually on gaiter area; no fever, less tenderness; responds to topical steroids, not antibiotics
- **Gout or pseudogout:** acute monoarticular swelling, extreme pain, red over joint — check urate, consider aspiration
- **Contact dermatitis (e.g., from dressings, emollients):** itching, blistering, well-defined border; recent topical product use
- **Erysipelas:** well-demarcated, raised, bright red plaque, often on face/ear — same treatment as cellulitis
- **Lymphoedema (acute flare):** pitting oedema not resolving with elevation, + Stemmer sign (cannot pinch dorsal foot); no fever unless superinfected

**Splitter:** Unilateral redness + pain + fever → cellulitis; bilateral + itching + no fever → venous stasis dermatitis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** check for localised abscess (fluctuance, ultrasound if needed); mark erythema border with a pen to monitor progression; measure ankle-brachial index (ABPI) if foot pulses absent to rule out critical ischaemia mimicking infection
- **Bloods:** FBC (WBC, neutrophils), CRP, U&E (renal function for antibiotic dosing), lactate, glucose (diabetes risk), blood cultures only if systemically unwell or immunocompromised; consider creatinine kinase (CK) if concern for NF/myositis
- **Imaging:**
    - First-line: ultrasound if abscess suspected; CT/MRI if NF suspected (gas, fascial thickening)
    - Second-line: X-ray if foreign body or gas (but gas can be absent early)
- **Special tests:** skin swab only if weeping/pus; not routinely for intact skin
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:** None validated for cellulitis severity — use clinical judgment (Eron classification: Class 1: no systemic signs; Class 2: systemically unwell but no organ failure; Class 3: severe sepsis; Class 4: septic shock)
- **Never-miss test:** **Lactate + NEWS2** — within first 15 minutes to screen for sepsis; also **assess pain out of proportion** to rule out NF
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** assess for sepsis – if NEWS2 ≥5, follow local sepsis protocol (IV antibiotics, fluids, lactate, blood cultures, senior review). If pain out of proportion, **immediate surgical review** for NF. Mark erythema border with permanent marker. Analgesia: paracetamol (if mild), oral opioids if needed.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** IV antibiotics if moderate/severe (see Drugs section). Elevate limb to reduce oedema (important for antibiotic delivery). Monitor erythema progression (re-mark border every 6-12h). Re-check CRP at 48h; if not falling, reconsider diagnosis / check for abscess. If penicillin allergy, choose clindamycin or vancomycin. Consider switching to oral when improving (CRP falling, fever resolved, border stable).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Eron Class 1 (no systemic signs, mild) can be managed entirely oral at home if compliance reliable. Ensure daily review of border if possible (GP, community nurse). Duration: 7 days typical; extend to 14 days if severe or slower response. Address predisposing factors: treat tinea pedis (interdigital fungal infection) with topical antifungal; manage lymphoedema with compression stockings once infection resolved; diabetic foot ulcer – refer early to podiatry/multidisciplinary foot team.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 500 mL-1L bolus if septic, then maintenance (25-30 mL/kg/day) depending on renal function
    - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (active bleed, etc.)
    - Diet: no restrictions (NBM only if needing surgery)
    - Observations: NEWS2 minimum 4-hourly; if severe sepsis, consider 1-2 hourly initially
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (mild – oral):** Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS PO for 7 days (BNF)
- **First-line (moderate/severe – IV):** Flucloxacillin 1 g QDS IV (consider 2 g if high body weight or severe sepsis)
- **Penicillin allergy (non-anaphylaxis):** Cefalexin 500 mg QDS PO (caution in immediate hypersensitivity – use alternative)
- **Penicillin anaphylaxis or severe allergy:** Clindamycin 300-450 mg QDS PO (IV: 600 mg QDS) – covers Strep and Staph, but check local resistance. If MRSA risk (known colonisation, recent hospitalisation, IVDU): consider adding Daptomycin or Vancomycin – [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Flucloxacillin can cause cholestatic jaundice (especially in elderly, long course) – check LFTs; avoid in history of penicillin anaphylaxis. Clindamycin: risk of *Clostridioides difficile* infection – monitor diarrhoea. Cefalexin: avoid in immediate penicillin allergy (risk cross-reactivity 1-10%).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Flucloxacillin – no dose adjustment in renal impairment; caution in hepatic impairment (monitor LFTs). Cefalexin – adjust if eGFR <10 mL/min (dose reduction). Clindamycin – no adjustment needed unless severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving an incorrect (under)dose of flucloxacillin oral** — many patients need 1g QDS for adequate tissue penetration, especially in lower limb with oedema. **Using clindamycin monotherapy for penicillin anaphylaxis** without checking for MRSA risks (if high risk, must add Gram-negative/MRSA coverage). **Prescribing clindamycin without considering C. diff –** use only if truly indicated.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Abscess formation (needs drainage), lymphangitis, bacteraemia/sepsis, necrotising fasciitis (rare but deadly), acute kidney injury from dehydration/sepsis
- **Chronic:** Recurrent cellulitis (especially due to untreated lymphoedema/tinea pedis), chronic venous insufficiency flare, lymphoedema worsening, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent if uncomplicated – most improve within 48-72h; recurrence rate 15-30% within 2 years if predisposing conditions not managed.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Mark the erythema border** at baseline – ask nursing staff to do the same each shift to objectively track progression.
- **Check for interdigital fissures (athlete's foot)** – treat with topical clotrimazole/miconazole; this can prevent recurrence.
- **Consider prophylactic antibiotics** (e.g., penicillin V 250 mg BD PO) if >2 episodes per year – refer to dermatology/infectious diseases.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give steroids** for the swelling – will worsen infection unless confirmed venous stasis eczema.
- **Do not apply topical antibiotics** to intact skin – no benefit; use only if weeping/pus (then consider swab).
- **Do not discharge without addressing predisposing factors** – recurrence is common; arrange follow-up with GP for lymphoedema/compression.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening redness beyond the marked line (especially if extends >3cm in 24h), new high fever or chills, severe pain, spreading red streaks, inability to tolerate oral antibiotics, new confusion
- **Follow-up:** GP review at 48-72h to reassess; if no improvement, admit or consider IV antibiotics in community (OPAT if available)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:** lactate >4 mmol/L, NEWS2 ≥5, GCS <15, new hypotension (SBP <90), new confusion, anuria, respiratory distress.
- **Consider surgical consult (acute/vascular) if:** pain out of proportion, suspected necrotising fasciitis (gas on imaging, rapid spread), compartment syndrome, or failure to respond after 48h of appropriate IV antibiotics.

rawMarkdown:
## Cellulitis — Lower Limb

**One-liner:** Acute bacterial skin infection of the lower limb; common but can mimic limb-threatening conditions (necrotising fasciitis, DVT) and cause sepsis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Necrotising fasciitis (NF):** extreme pain out of proportion to skin changes, rapid progression, systemic toxicity, bullae/haemorrhagic blisters, gas on palpation/imaging. **The single finding:** pain *worse* than the visible skin suggests NF — this is the killer, not simple cellulitis.
- **Sepsis with shock:** high NEWS2, lactate >2 mmol/L, hypotension not responding to fluids. **The single finding:** lactate >4 + HR >110 despite 20 mL/kg fluid — needs urgent antibiotics and senior input.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** acute onset of unilateral lower limb redness, warmth, swelling, pain; often with fever/chills; usually no wound visible (but look for interdigital cracks, ulcers, trauma)
- **Key exam findings:** erythema with poorly defined border (vs well-defined in erysipelas); local warmth; tender to palpation; ± lymphangitic streaking or inguinal lymphadenopathy; check for bullae, pus, weeping; measure limb circumference for progression

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Deep vein thrombosis (DVT):** purple-red, non-tender swelling, Homan's sign unreliable — use Wells score (DVT likely >2 points); do D-dimer + ultrasound if low pretest probability
- **Venous stasis dermatitis (eczema):** bilateral, itchy, scaly, usually on gaiter area; no fever, less tenderness; responds to topical steroids, not antibiotics
- **Gout or pseudogout:** acute monoarticular swelling, extreme pain, red over joint — check urate, consider aspiration
- **Contact dermatitis (e.g., from dressings, emollients):** itching, blistering, well-defined border; recent topical product use
- **Erysipelas:** well-demarcated, raised, bright red plaque, often on face/ear — same treatment as cellulitis
- **Lymphoedema (acute flare):** pitting oedema not resolving with elevation, + Stemmer sign (cannot pinch dorsal foot); no fever unless superinfected

**Splitter:** Unilateral redness + pain + fever → cellulitis; bilateral + itching + no fever → venous stasis dermatitis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** check for localised abscess (fluctuance, ultrasound if needed); mark erythema border with a pen to monitor progression; measure ankle-brachial index (ABPI) if foot pulses absent to rule out critical ischaemia mimicking infection
- **Bloods:** FBC (WBC, neutrophils), CRP, U&E (renal function for antibiotic dosing), lactate, glucose (diabetes risk), blood cultures only if systemically unwell or immunocompromised; consider creatinine kinase (CK) if concern for NF/myositis
- **Imaging:**
    - First-line: ultrasound if abscess suspected; CT/MRI if NF suspected (gas, fascial thickening)
    - Second-line: X-ray if foreign body or gas (but gas can be absent early)
- **Special tests:** skin swab only if weeping/pus; not routinely for intact skin
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:** None validated for cellulitis severity — use clinical judgment (Eron classification: Class 1: no systemic signs; Class 2: systemically unwell but no organ failure; Class 3: severe sepsis; Class 4: septic shock)
- **Never-miss test:** **Lactate + NEWS2** — within first 15 minutes to screen for sepsis; also **assess pain out of proportion** to rule out NF

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** assess for sepsis – if NEWS2 ≥5, follow local sepsis protocol (IV antibiotics, fluids, lactate, blood cultures, senior review). If pain out of proportion, **immediate surgical review** for NF. Mark erythema border with permanent marker. Analgesia: paracetamol (if mild), oral opioids if needed.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** IV antibiotics if moderate/severe (see Drugs section). Elevate limb to reduce oedema (important for antibiotic delivery). Monitor erythema progression (re-mark border every 6-12h). Re-check CRP at 48h; if not falling, reconsider diagnosis / check for abscess. If penicillin allergy, choose clindamycin or vancomycin. Consider switching to oral when improving (CRP falling, fever resolved, border stable).
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Eron Class 1 (no systemic signs, mild) can be managed entirely oral at home if compliance reliable. Ensure daily review of border if possible (GP, community nurse). Duration: 7 days typical; extend to 14 days if severe or slower response. Address predisposing factors: treat tinea pedis (interdigital fungal infection) with topical antifungal; manage lymphoedema with compression stockings once infection resolved; diabetic foot ulcer – refer early to podiatry/multidisciplinary foot team.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 500 mL-1L bolus if septic, then maintenance (25-30 mL/kg/day) depending on renal function
    - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (active bleed, etc.)
    - Diet: no restrictions (NBM only if needing surgery)
    - Observations: NEWS2 minimum 4-hourly; if severe sepsis, consider 1-2 hourly initially

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (mild – oral):** Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS PO for 7 days (BNF)
- **First-line (moderate/severe – IV):** Flucloxacillin 1 g QDS IV (consider 2 g if high body weight or severe sepsis)
- **Penicillin allergy (non-anaphylaxis):** Cefalexin 500 mg QDS PO (caution in immediate hypersensitivity – use alternative)
- **Penicillin anaphylaxis or severe allergy:** Clindamycin 300-450 mg QDS PO (IV: 600 mg QDS) – covers Strep and Staph, but check local resistance. If MRSA risk (known colonisation, recent hospitalisation, IVDU): consider adding Daptomycin or Vancomycin – [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** Flucloxacillin can cause cholestatic jaundice (especially in elderly, long course) – check LFTs; avoid in history of penicillin anaphylaxis. Clindamycin: risk of *Clostridioides difficile* infection – monitor diarrhoea. Cefalexin: avoid in immediate penicillin allergy (risk cross-reactivity 1-10%).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Flucloxacillin – no dose adjustment in renal impairment; caution in hepatic impairment (monitor LFTs). Cefalexin – adjust if eGFR <10 mL/min (dose reduction). Clindamycin – no adjustment needed unless severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving an incorrect (under)dose of flucloxacillin oral** — many patients need 1g QDS for adequate tissue penetration, especially in lower limb with oedema. **Using clindamycin monotherapy for penicillin anaphylaxis** without checking for MRSA risks (if high risk, must add Gram-negative/MRSA coverage). **Prescribing clindamycin without considering C. diff –** use only if truly indicated.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Abscess formation (needs drainage), lymphangitis, bacteraemia/sepsis, necrotising fasciitis (rare but deadly), acute kidney injury from dehydration/sepsis
- **Chronic:** Recurrent cellulitis (especially due to untreated lymphoedema/tinea pedis), chronic venous insufficiency flare, lymphoedema worsening, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation

### Prognosis
- Excellent if uncomplicated – most improve within 48-72h; recurrence rate 15-30% within 2 years if predisposing conditions not managed.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Mark the erythema border** at baseline – ask nursing staff to do the same each shift to objectively track progression.
- **Check for interdigital fissures (athlete's foot)** – treat with topical clotrimazole/miconazole; this can prevent recurrence.
- **Consider prophylactic antibiotics** (e.g., penicillin V 250 mg BD PO) if >2 episodes per year – refer to dermatology/infectious diseases.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give steroids** for the swelling – will worsen infection unless confirmed venous stasis eczema.
- **Do not apply topical antibiotics** to intact skin – no benefit; use only if weeping/pus (then consider swab).
- **Do not discharge without addressing predisposing factors** – recurrence is common; arrange follow-up with GP for lymphoedema/compression.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening redness beyond the marked line (especially if extends >3cm in 24h), new high fever or chills, severe pain, spreading red streaks, inability to tolerate oral antibiotics, new confusion
- **Follow-up:** GP review at 48-72h to reassess; if no improvement, admit or consider IV antibiotics in community (OPAT if available)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:** lactate >4 mmol/L, NEWS2 ≥5, GCS <15, new hypotension (SBP <90), new confusion, anuria, respiratory distress.
- **Consider surgical consult (acute/vascular) if:** pain out of proportion, suspected necrotising fasciitis (gas on imaging, rapid spread), compartment syndrome, or failure to respond after 48h of appropriate IV antibiotics.
